title: Car Periscope

jokes.text: You upstream me? Everybody upstreams, you idiot. You don't stand there like a cab gas.
jokes.setup: Two people fighting over a taxi cab
jokes.punchline: Larry's indignant reaction and made-up term 'cab gas' for someone who stands passively waiting for cabs
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry - takes a minor social situation and creates his own terminology and rules. 'Cab gas' is inventive wordplay that fits his character perfectly.

jokes.text: Yeah, right. That's why you married Susie? 'Cause she doesn't tell you what to do.
jokes.setup: Jeff says he doesn't like trainers because he doesn't like being told what to do
jokes.punchline: Larry points out the irony that Jeff is married to the notoriously controlling Susie
jokes.explanation: Sharp character-based irony that fans of the show would immediately recognize. Economical and well-structured.

jokes.text: I stink.
jokes.setup: Jeff has been exercising/training
jokes.punchline: Blunt, matter-of-fact admission
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy moment but lacks originality or significant comedic impact.

jokes.text: A ski that comes in two pieces. You screw it on, okay? Like a pool cue.
jokes.setup: Jeff claims to be an inventor with ideas
jokes.punchline: His 'invention' is a collapsible ski that screws together
jokes.explanation: Establishes Jeff's delusional self-image as an 'inventor.' The absurdity of the idea and his earnestness makes it funny.

jokes.text: I'm an idea man who comes up with inventions. No, there are no inventions in your world.
jokes.setup: Debate over whether Jeff is an inventor or just has ideas
jokes.punchline: Larry's brutal dismissal of Jeff's entire 'world' having no actual inventions
jokes.explanation: Larry's characteristic bluntness in deflating Jeff's pretensions. The phrase 'in your world' adds comedic condescension.

jokes.text: This is where I lost my virginity-- On this block. Susan Fleschner, swear to God.
jokes.setup: Casual conversation while walking
jokes.punchline: Larry's sudden, unprompted oversharing about losing his virginity
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry inappropriate oversharing. The specificity of the name and location adds authenticity to the cringe.

jokes.text: I've actually thought of that. I've been in cars and I wanted-- You know, I'm stuck in traffic and I'm thinking there should be a periscope.
jokes.setup: Inventor shows them a car periscope
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately claims he had the same idea, typical of his need to insert himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry behavior - can't let someone else have credit for an idea without claiming he thought of it first.

jokes.text: Do you know a kid once stabbed me in class? With the pencil side, thank God. That side-- I wouldn't be here looking at your invention.
jokes.setup: Looking at a compass with sharp points
jokes.punchline: Larry's casual mention of being stabbed in class, with gratitude it was the blunt end
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered casually. The matter-of-fact way Larry mentions childhood trauma is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: You know, when people go skiing, the ski's, like, really long, right? I'm thinking like a ski that you screw in like a pool cue
jokes.setup: Jeff tries to pitch his invention to the real inventor
jokes.punchline: He pitches the exact same ski idea we heard earlier, oblivious to how it sounds
jokes.explanation: Great callback that shows Jeff's complete lack of self-awareness. The repetition makes his delusion even funnier.

jokes.text: This could be a big, elaborate con game for all we know. You think it could be a con game? You know, look at 'The Sting.' That's elaborate.
jokes.setup: Larry questions whether they should trust the inventor
jokes.punchline: References 'The Sting' as evidence that elaborate cons exist
jokes.explanation: Sets up the bigger joke about seeing sequels before originals. Larry's paranoid logic is characteristic.

jokes.text: I never saw it. I saw 'The Sting II.' You didn't see 'The Sting'? No, but 'The Sting II,' Jackie Gleason-- pretty good.
jokes.setup: Larry mentions 'The Sting' movie
jokes.punchline: Jeff reveals he saw the sequel but never the original
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic that only someone like Jeff would follow. The specificity of Jackie Gleason makes it even funnier.

jokes.text: What kind of idiot sees 'Sting II' and not 'Sting I'? You must be the only person in the world who saw 'Sting II' and not 'Sting I.'
jokes.setup: Jeff's admission about seeing the sequel first
jokes.punchline: Larry's incredulous escalation, making Jeff seem uniquely stupid
jokes.explanation: Larry's characteristic outrage at Jeff's illogical behavior. The escalation to 'only person in the world' is perfectly Larry.

jokes.text: Any questions I had about this guy were just answered. He's smart. He's good-looking. He can have anybody. He chose her. That tells you everything you need to know. This guy's got integrity.
jokes.setup: Larry and Jeff meet the inventor's wife
jokes.punchline: Larry judges the inventor's character based on marrying an unattractive woman
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly twisted Larry logic - using someone's choice in spouse as a business assessment. Dark but character-perfect reasoning.

jokes.text: You two are a couple of schmucks, you know that? This looks like an eighth-grade science experiment, this thing.
jokes.setup: Larry and Jeff are excited about the car periscope prototype
jokes.punchline: Susie's brutal assessment of their intelligence and the invention
jokes.explanation: Perfect Susie - cutting through their excitement with harsh reality. 'Eighth-grade science experiment' is a perfect insult.

jokes.text: We've got a garbage truck at 12,00. You go to your left. Go to your left. You really see something? Yeah. Oh! Oh my God! Look at this!
jokes.setup: Testing the car periscope in traffic
jokes.punchline: Their genuine excitement at seeing what's ahead through the periscope
jokes.explanation: Their childlike wonder at the periscope working creates genuine comedy through character reaction and escalating excitement.

jokes.text: Wanda's place in L.A. is haunted. That's why she's out here. But now New York is haunted. Why? 'Cause he's here? Right?
jokes.setup: Wanda explains why she's in New York instead of L.A.
jokes.punchline: She implies Larry is what makes places 'haunted'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that transforms 'haunted' from supernatural to Larry-related. Economical and character-specific insult.

jokes.text: I'm still at the same email-- ihatelarry.
jokes.setup: Wanda says Larry should email her trainer's info
jokes.punchline: Her email address is literally 'ihatelarry'
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to their relationship dynamic. The casual delivery makes it funnier - like it's the most natural email address ever.

jokes.text: I happen to be in top physical shape. Top physical shape? Yes, I am. As compared to what?
jokes.setup: Larry claims to be in top physical shape
jokes.punchline: Wanda's devastating follow-up question
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/punchline structure. Wanda's 'compared to what' completely deflates Larry's boast with surgical precision.

jokes.text: You know who his dad is, right? No. Judge Horn. Your dad's Judge Horn? Oh my God, I love Judge Horn.
jokes.setup: Meeting someone whose father is a TV judge
jokes.punchline: Larry's escalating excitement about the TV show
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Judge Horn storyline. Larry's fanboy excitement is mildly amusing but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I would laugh for hours about him screaming at the defendants-- 'You're not prepared, young lady! Where are the pictures?'
jokes.setup: Larry explaining his love for Judge Horn
jokes.punchline: His impression of the judge's courtroom antics
jokes.explanation: Larry's enthusiasm for trashy TV shows rings true. The specific courtroom dialogue adds authenticity.

jokes.text: I always think of nice things but I never act on them. What is that? It's like you with inventions, You think of inventions; You never do the inventions. I don't implement.
jokes.setup: Larry wants to do something nice for the judge
jokes.punchline: Larry compares his procrastination to Jeff's lack of invention follow-through
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that creates parallel character flaws. Larry's self-awareness is unusually honest for him.

jokes.text: I always think of nice things but I never act on them. What is that? It's like you with inventions. You think of inventions; You never do the inventions. / I don't implement.
jokes.setup: Larry wants to do something nice for the judge
jokes.punchline: Larry's self-analysis paralleled with Jeff's invention problem
jokes.explanation: Great character insight and callback to the implementer concept. Shows Larry's rare self-awareness.

jokes.text: He has to announce it. He can't just do something nice. He has to announce it.
jokes.setup: Larry announces he's going to do something nice
jokes.punchline: Susie's commentary on Larry's need to announce his good deeds
jokes.explanation: Susie perfectly captures Larry's character flaw - his need for credit even for basic kindness. Sharp character observation.

jokes.text: People are confused when they shake hands-- Lefty, righty. Sometimes I wish I had one arm when I'm cuddling in bed with a woman, 'cause the other arm gets in the way all the time
jokes.setup: Discussion about handshaking with one arm
jokes.punchline: The one-armed man's TMI about bedroom logistics
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about disability that works because it comes from the character himself. The oversharing makes it awkward in a funny way.

jokes.text: A lot of kikes liked the show, jigaboos too.
jokes.setup: Larry mentions his father liked Judge Horn's show
jokes.punchline: Judge Horn's casual use of racial slurs
jokes.explanation: Shocking moment that creates maximum cringe. The casual delivery makes it more disturbing and funny through sheer uncomfortable surprise.

jokes.text: 217 to 67? Can I talk to you outside for a second, Larry?
jokes.setup: Larry supposedly playing Scrabble with the elderly judge
jokes.punchline: The impossibly lopsided score suggests Larry destroyed an old man at Scrabble
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual gag - the score alone tells the story of Larry's apparent cruelty to an elderly man. Economical storytelling.

jokes.text: Taking advantage of my father at scrabble? I didn't play him. I wasn't playing him. You weren't playing him? Who else was there, Larry? I took over for a one-armed man.
jokes.setup: Henry accuses Larry of beating his elderly father at Scrabble
jokes.punchline: Larry's increasingly desperate claim about a one-armed man
jokes.explanation: The one-armed man becomes Larry's increasingly absurd excuse. Sets up the episode's central running gag perfectly.

jokes.text: Yes, he had a work uniform like he was fixing an air conditioner. Larry, Larry, I'm having a party. We don't have any maintenance people up here doing work.
jokes.setup: Larry tries to describe the one-armed man to prove he exists
jokes.punchline: Henry's logical response that there's no maintenance work being done
jokes.explanation: Larry's specific details make his lie seem more desperate. Henry's logical response exposes the absurdity.

jokes.text: Did anybody see a one-armed man? There was a one-armed man here. Larry, there's no one-armed man.
jokes.setup: Larry desperately tries to find witnesses to the one-armed man
jokes.punchline: His public plea falls flat with no one backing him up
jokes.explanation: Larry's public desperation escalates the situation. The visual of him polling party guests is inherently funny.

jokes.text: My father has dementia, Larry. Oh, that's an excuse? Yes. That racism is in there somewhere. It's like a drunk-- What, they're saying these things because they're drunk? No, it's in there.
jokes.setup: Henry explains his father's racism is due to dementia
jokes.punchline: Larry's controversial theory that dementia just reveals existing racism
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly uncomfortable Larry logic that's both offensive and oddly thought-provoking. His drunk analogy is perfectly Larry reasoning.

jokes.text: My grandfather had dementia. He wasn't a racist. He thought I was his dead sister, but he wasn't a racist.
jokes.setup: Discussion about dementia and racism
jokes.punchline: Jeff's grandfather confused Jeff for his dead sister but maintained his non-racist values
jokes.explanation: Funny counter-example to Larry's theory. The specificity of being mistaken for a dead sister adds absurdist humor.

jokes.text: Did you look like your great-aunt? You know what? I actually did. I swear to God, I looked exactly like her. She was the belle of Brighton.
jokes.setup: Jeff's grandfather confused him for his dead sister
jokes.punchline: Jeff admits he actually did look like her and she was beautiful
jokes.explanation: Jeff's pride in resembling the 'belle of Brighton' adds another layer of absurd character detail.

jokes.text: Oh my-- Jeff, it's the one-armed man. I swear to God. He's crossing the street. Check it out. I see one arm. Are you sure that's the right one-armed man?
jokes.setup: Using the periscope in traffic
jokes.punchline: Larry spots what he claims is THE one-armed man through the periscope
jokes.explanation: Brilliant convergence of the periscope and one-armed man storylines. Jeff's logical question adds to the comedy.

jokes.text: We'll head him off at the pass.
jokes.setup: Chasing the one-armed man through the city
jokes.punchline: Larry uses Western movie terminology for their urban pursuit
jokes.explanation: Larry's Western movie reference in this urban context adds absurdist flavor to their chase sequence.

jokes.text: I had a client-- He threw away millions on a two-part ski that would come together like a pool cue.
jokes.setup: Business manager explaining why inventions are bad investments
jokes.punchline: He unknowingly describes Jeff's exact invention idea
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that creates perfect ironic timing. The audience knows it's Jeff's idea while Nathan unknowingly condemns it.

jokes.text: Are you serious? That's my idea. I had that idea. People's legs would snap like that.
jokes.setup: Nathan describes the failed ski invention
jokes.punchline: Larry reveals it was his idea, and Nathan predicts injury
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to the ski invention running gag. Nathan's prediction of injury adds dark humor.

jokes.text: That's your wife? Yes. Oh my God. I don't think we can work together anymore.
jokes.setup: Larry meets Nathan's beautiful wife
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately decides he can't work with Nathan because of it
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Larry's wife-assessment theory, now applied in reverse. His consistency is hilariously absurd.

jokes.text: She's beautiful. Yes, thank you. I don't like what that says about you. What does it say about me? It says I have good judgment. It says you're a very superficial man. Superficial? No depth.
jokes.setup: Larry explains why Nathan's beautiful wife is a problem
jokes.punchline: Larry's twisted logic about superficiality and Nathan's confused defense
jokes.explanation: Larry's warped moral logic is perfectly in character. Nathan's reasonable confusion makes Larry seem even more insane.

jokes.text: Can I honestly say you are the first inventor I've ever handed a check to? Me too. Me too.
jokes.setup: Larry and Jeff decide to invest in the car periscope
jokes.punchline: They both admit they've never invested in an inventor before
jokes.explanation: Their mutual admission highlights their inexperience, adding to the sense they're making a mistake.

jokes.text: You know, I was actually planning on taking that dish home with me. Oh yeah. Well, not now.
jokes.setup: Larry eats food that Gaby was planning to take home
jokes.punchline: Gaby's passive-aggressive response
jokes.explanation: Standard awkward moment but not particularly clever. Sets up tension but doesn't pay off strongly.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm feeling faint. I'm feeling faint, hold on. I just need a shoulder to rest on for a second.
jokes.setup: Gaby suddenly feels faint during conversation
jokes.punchline: She needs Larry's shoulder for support in what seems like a staged moment
jokes.explanation: Weak setup that feels contrived. The physical comedy aspect doesn't land particularly well.

jokes.text: Oh, that was my girlfriend. She seems lovely. She is-- She is the best. I can tell. You guys-- I'm gonna marry her if she'll have me.
jokes.setup: Henry sees Larry with a woman he thinks is Larry's girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Larry goes along with it, claiming he'll marry 'her'
jokes.explanation: Larry's willingness to maintain a false impression for social advantage is very in character. Sets up his reputation redemption.

jokes.text: Larry, it's so clear that I misjudged you. And I can see now you are a man of great character.
jokes.setup: Henry sees Larry with what he thinks is an unattractive girlfriend
jokes.punchline: He completely reverses his opinion of Larry's character
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic payoff - Larry's superficial theory works in his favor when applied incorrectly. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: Had? That's all you can think of-- Had? I guess I'm just not a very good player. I'm doing the best I can. I can't believe that. I'm sorry. I'm just not that good.
jokes.setup: Larry playing Scrabble with the racist judge
jokes.punchline: Larry deliberately plays poorly, offering only simple words like 'had'
jokes.explanation: Larry's strategy to avoid destroying the elderly man creates its own awkwardness. His fake incompetence is believable.

jokes.text: C-o-o-n. Coon? Coon. Okay, that's not a word, all right? Oh, it is. That's a racial epithet. That's not a word. Oh no? Just look around you. They're all over the park.
jokes.setup: Judge plays what appears to be a racial slur in Scrabble
jokes.punchline: He's actually referring to raccoons in the park
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection that subverts our expectations. We expect racism but get actual raccoons. The tension and release is perfectly crafted.

jokes.text: We're surrounded, and after I put half of them away. Okay, Judge. You know what? I'm gonna give you 'coon.' That's big of you.
jokes.setup: Judge Horn talking about raccoons
jokes.punchline: Larry's grudging acceptance and the Judge's understated response
jokes.explanation: Great follow-up that maintains the misdirection comedy. 'That's big of you' is perfectly deadpan.

jokes.text: Who would you rather run around the park with, huh? Just-- To be totally honest, Larry, she's paying me a little bit more.
jokes.setup: Wanda challenges Larry about who Terry prefers
jokes.punchline: Terry admits it's purely about money
jokes.explanation: Brutally honest response that deflates any pretense. Terry's matter-of-fact admission adds awkward humor.

jokes.text: You know Judge Horn from TV? Oh yeah. He's an impartial third party. Why don't we let him decide? He's neutral.
jokes.setup: Larry and Wanda need to resolve their trainer dispute
jokes.punchline: Larry suggests the racist judge as an 'impartial' arbitrator
jokes.explanation: Brilliant ironic setup - Larry suggests the racist judge as 'neutral' to judge between him and a Black woman. Perfect dark comedy setup.

jokes.text: You're gonna take your popcorn into the bathroom? That's disgusting. No no no, I hold on to it. I actually-- I munch and pee.
jokes.setup: Ira wants to take his popcorn to the bathroom
jokes.punchline: He admits to 'munching and peeing' simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Disgusting admission that creates visceral comedy. 'Munch and pee' is memorably gross phrasing.

jokes.text: Because what, you think I'm gonna eat your popcorn? Are you worried she's gonna eat your popcorn? Are you crazy? I'm really uncomfortable with this.
jokes.setup: Larry offers to hold Ira's popcorn
jokes.punchline: Larry escalates to accusing Ira of not trusting him, creating more tension
jokes.explanation: Larry's ability to turn a simple gesture into a major confrontation is perfectly in character. The escalation feels natural.

jokes.text: This is really bad. You can take your check. Are you nuts? I'm a man of integrity. Yeah, I know. Obviously. Obviously? What's that? What the fuck does that mean?
jokes.setup: Business deal falls apart over popcorn incident
jokes.punchline: Larry's 'obviously' comment about integrity triggers Ira's rage
jokes.explanation: Larry's casual 'obviously' is interpreted as sarcastic, escalating the situation. His social blindness creates perfect conflict.

jokes.text: Oh, that ski idea. Yeah, it was unbelievable. How stupid was that? A lot of money lost.
jokes.setup: New business manager discussing bad investments
jokes.punchline: He unknowingly insults Larry's ski idea to Larry himself
jokes.explanation: Another great callback to the ski invention. The irony of insulting Larry's idea to his face adds comedic tension.

jokes.text: That's your wife? Yep, met in college-- 20 years. You're hired.
jokes.setup: Larry meets the new business manager's plain wife
jokes.punchline: He immediately hires him based solely on his wife's appearance
jokes.explanation: Perfect culmination of the running gag. Larry's instant decision based purely on superficial wife-assessment is brilliantly absurd and consistent.

jokes.text: I've been looking for you. You got me in a lot of trouble with that scrabble game. What were you doing in that apartment anyway? None of your fucking business.
jokes.setup: Larry finally confronts the one-armed man
jokes.punchline: The one-armed man is hostile and evasive about being at the party
jokes.explanation: Payoff to the running gag, though the one-armed man's hostility suggests there's more to the story. Sets up the final reveal.

jokes.text: Are you upstreaming me? You can't upstream me. Up what? What are you--? I was here first.
jokes.setup: Larry and the one-armed man fight over a taxi
jokes.punchline: Larry uses his 'upstreaming' terminology while the one-armed man is confused
jokes.explanation: Nice bookend callback to the opening scene's taxi conflict and Larry's invented 'upstreaming' term.

jokes.text: Dad! Dad, oh my God. Dad! Who did this to you? The one-armed man!
jokes.setup: Henry finds his father injured
jokes.punchline: Judge identifies Larry's one-armed man as the attacker
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode-ending callback that vindicates Larry's story while creating new questions. The dramatic reveal is both funny and shocking.

tmdb_overview: Larry and Jeff weigh an investment opportunity; Wanda Sykes preempts Larry’s training schedule.
